description: This section covers sepsis management, from resuscitation and fluids to vasopressors and

trials.full_entry: ANDROMEDA-SHOCK (Hernández et al., 2019): Compared a resuscitation strategy guided by peripheral perfusion (capillary refill time) to one guided by lactate clearance, finding no significant difference but a trend towards better outcomes with the perfusion-guided strategy.
trials.description: Compared a resuscitation strategy guided by peripheral perfusion (capillary refill time) to one guided by lactate clearance, finding no significant difference but a trend towards better outcomes with the perfusion-guided strategy.

trials.full_entry: CLASSIC (Meyhoff et al., 2022): In ICU patients with septic shock, restricting IV fluids after initial resuscitation did not result in fewer deaths at 90 days compared to a standard fluid strategy.
trials.description: In ICU patients with septic shock, restricting IV fluids after initial resuscitation did not result in fewer deaths at 90 days compared to a standard fluid strategy.

trials.full_entry: CLOVERS (The PETAL Clinical Trials Network, 2023): In patients with sepsis- induced hypotension, a restrictive fluid strategy (prioritizing vasopressors) did not result in a significant difference in 90-day mortality compared to a liberal fluid strategy.
trials.description: In patients with sepsis- induced hypotension, a restrictive fluid strategy (prioritizing vasopressors) did not result in a significant difference in 90-day mortality compared to a liberal fluid strategy.

trials.full_entry: EGDT (Rivers et al., 2001): The original trial that showed a mortality benefit with protocolized Early Goal-Directed Therapy (EGDT) for severe sepsis and septic shock, setting the standard of care for over a decade.
trials.description: The original trial that showed a mortality benefit with protocolized Early Goal-Directed Therapy (EGDT) for severe sepsis and septic shock, setting the standard of care for over a decade.

trials.full_entry: Jones et al. (2017) (CENSER Trial): Showed that early initiation of norepinephrine in the Emergency Department for septic shock patients led to better shock control and less fluid administration.
trials.description: Showed that early initiation of norepinephrine in the Emergency Department for septic shock patients led to better shock control and less fluid administration.

trials.full_entry: KUMAR (Kumar et al., 2006): Landmark observational study showing that each hour of delay in administering effective antibiotics after the onset of septic shock was associated with a significant increase in mortality.
trials.description: Landmark observational study showing that each hour of delay in administering effective antibiotics after the onset of septic shock was associated with a significant increase in mortality.

trials.full_entry: Morelli et al (2013): A trial on the use of the beta-blocker esmolol to control heart rate in patients with septic shock, which showed improved heart rate control but no mortality benefit.
trials.description: A trial on the use of the beta-blocker esmolol to control heart rate in patients with septic shock, which showed improved heart rate control but no mortality benefit.

trials.full_entry: ProCESS (2014), ARISE (2014), & ProMISe (2015): This trio of large, multicenter trials collectively demonstrated that protocol-based EGDT was not superior to usual care for septic shock, effectively ending the EGDT era.
trials.description: This trio of large, multicenter trials collectively demonstrated that protocol-based EGDT was not superior to usual care for septic shock, effectively ending the EGDT era.

trials.full_entry: WATERFALL (de-Bruyn et al., 2022): In patients with septic shock, an early de- resuscitation strategy (active fluid removal) after initial resuscitation did not improve outcomes compared to usual care.
trials.description: In patients with septic shock, an early de- resuscitation strategy (active fluid removal) after initial resuscitation did not improve outcomes compared to usual care.

trials.full_entry: 65 Trial (Lamontagne et al., 2020): In older ICU patients (≥65 years) with vasodilatory shock, permissive hypotension (targeting MAP of 60-65 mmHg) was not different from usual care.
trials.description: In older ICU patients (≥65 years) with vasodilatory shock, permissive hypotension (targeting MAP of 60-65 mmHg) was not different from usual care.

trials.full_entry: ATHOS-3 (Khanna et al., 2017): In patients with distributive shock refractory to high-dose vasopressors, angiotensin II effectively increased blood pressure.
trials.description: In patients with distributive shock refractory to high-dose vasopressors, angiotensin II effectively increased blood pressure.

trials.full_entry: SEPSISPAM (Asfar et al., 2014): Targeting a high MAP (80-85 mmHg) compared to a standard MAP (65-70 mmHg) did not improve survival in septic shock, but may have benefited patients with chronic hypertension.
trials.description: Targeting a high MAP (80-85 mmHg) compared to a standard MAP (65-70 mmHg) did not improve survival in septic shock, but may have benefited patients with chronic hypertension.

trials.full_entry: SOAP II (De Backer et al., 2010): Compared norepinephrine to dopamine as the first-line vasopressor in shock; norepinephrine was associated with fewer arrhythmias and a trend towards lower mortality.
trials.description: Compared norepinephrine to dopamine as the first-line vasopressor in shock; norepinephrine was associated with fewer arrhythmias and a trend towards lower mortality.

trials.full_entry: VANISH (Gordon et al., 2016): Compared norepinephrine to vasopressin as the initial vasopressor in septic shock, finding no difference in kidney failure-free days.
trials.description: Compared norepinephrine to vasopressin as the initial vasopressor in septic shock, finding no difference in kidney failure-free days.

trials.full_entry: VASST (Russell et al., 2008): In patients with septic shock on norepinephrine, the addition of low-dose vasopressin did not reduce overall mortality but may have benefited those with less severe shock.
trials.description: In patients with septic shock on norepinephrine, the addition of low-dose vasopressin did not reduce overall mortality but may have benefited those with less severe shock.

trials.full_entry: ADRENAL (Venkatesh et al., 2018): In septic shock, hydrocortisone infusion did not reduce 90-day mortality but resulted in faster shock resolution and shorter ICU stays.
trials.description: In septic shock, hydrocortisone infusion did not reduce 90-day mortality but resulted in faster shock resolution and shorter ICU stays.

trials.full_entry: Annane et al. (2002): An influential early trial suggesting a mortality benefit with low-dose corticosteroids in septic shock.
trials.description: An influential early trial suggesting a mortality benefit with low-dose corticosteroids in septic shock.

trials.full_entry: APROCCHSS (Annane et al., 2018): In septic shock, treatment with hydrocortisone plus fludrocortisone resulted in lower 90-day mortality compared to placebo.
trials.description: In septic shock, treatment with hydrocortisone plus fludrocortisone resulted in lower 90-day mortality compared to placebo.

trials.full_entry: CORTICUS (Sprung et al., 2008): Found that low-dose hydrocortisone did not improve survival in septic shock and led to more superinfections, challenging its routine use.
trials.description: Found that low-dose hydrocortisone did not improve survival in septic shock and led to more superinfections, challenging its routine use.

trials.full_entry: HYPRESS (Keh et al., 2016): Prophylactic hydrocortisone in patients with severe sepsis (but not yet shock) did not prevent the development of septic shock.
trials.description: Prophylactic hydrocortisone in patients with severe sepsis (but not yet shock) did not prevent the development of septic shock.

trials.full_entry: Sprung et al. (1984): One of the first influential trials suggesting a benefit with corticosteroids in septic shock.
trials.description: One of the first influential trials suggesting a benefit with corticosteroids in septic shock.

trials.full_entry: 6S (Perner et al., 2012) & CHEST (Myburgh et al., 2012): Two major trials that showed increased mortality and need for RRT with hydroxyethyl starch (HES) solutions compared to crystalloids in severe sepsis.
trials.description: Two major trials that showed increased mortality and need for RRT with hydroxyethyl starch (HES) solutions compared to crystalloids in severe sepsis.

trials.full_entry: ALBIOS (Caironi et al., 2014): In patients with severe sepsis, albumin replacement in addition to crystalloids did not improve survival compared to crystalloid resuscitation alone.
trials.description: In patients with severe sepsis, albumin replacement in addition to crystalloids did not improve survival compared to crystalloid resuscitation alone.

trials.full_entry: BaSICS (Zampieri et al., 2021) & PLUS (Finfer et al., 2022): Two very large ICU trials that found no significant difference in 90-day mortality between balanced crystalloids and saline for critically ill adults.
trials.description: Two very large ICU trials that found no significant difference in 90-day mortality between balanced crystalloids and saline for critically ill adults.

trials.full_entry: CRISTAL (Annane et al., 2013): Compared colloids to crystalloids in hypovolemic shock and found no difference in 28-day mortality.
trials.description: Compared colloids to crystalloids in hypovolemic shock and found no difference in 28-day mortality.

trials.full_entry: FEAST (Maitland et al., 2011): A landmark pediatric trial showing fluid boluses for septic children in resource-limited settings increased mortality.
trials.description: A landmark pediatric trial showing fluid boluses for septic children in resource-limited settings increased mortality.

trials.full_entry: FLUID Trial (Self et al., 2025): A hospital-wide trial showing that Lactated Ringer's did not result in a significant difference in 90-day death or hospital readmission compared with normal saline.
trials.description: A hospital-wide trial showing that Lactated Ringer's did not result in a significant difference in 90-day death or hospital readmission compared with normal saline.

trials.full_entry: SAFE (The SAFE Study Investigators, 2004): Compared 4% albumin to normal saline for resuscitation in a general ICU population and found no difference in mortality.
trials.description: Compared 4% albumin to normal saline for resuscitation in a general ICU population and found no difference in mortality.

trials.full_entry: SALT-ED (Self et al., 2018) & SMART (Semler et al., 2018): Found that balanced crystalloids resulted in a lower incidence of major adverse kidney events compared to saline in both ICU and ED populations.
trials.description: Found that balanced crystalloids resulted in a lower incidence of major adverse kidney events compared to saline in both ICU and ED populations.

trials.full_entry: SPLIT (Young et al., 2015): A large cluster-randomized trial that found no difference in acute kidney injury between a buffered crystalloid and saline.
trials.description: A large cluster-randomized trial that found no difference in acute kidney injury between a buffered crystalloid and saline.

trials.full_entry: BICAR-ICU (Jaber et al., 2018): In patients with severe metabolic acidemia (pH < 7.20), sodium bicarbonate infusion did not improve overall survival but did reduce the need for RRT in the subgroup with acute kidney injury.
trials.description: In patients with severe metabolic acidemia (pH < 7.20), sodium bicarbonate infusion did not improve overall survival but did reduce the need for RRT in the subgroup with acute kidney injury.

trials.full_entry: CITRIS-ALI (Fowler et al., 2019): High-dose intravenous vitamin C did not significantly improve organ failure scores or inflammatory biomarkers in patients with sepsis and ARDS.
trials.description: High-dose intravenous vitamin C did not significantly improve organ failure scores or inflammatory biomarkers in patients with sepsis and ARDS.

trials.full_entry: HYBERNATUS (Francois et al., 2015): Moderate hypothermia (32-34°C) did not reduce mortality in patients with septic shock.
trials.description: Moderate hypothermia (32-34°C) did not reduce mortality in patients with septic shock.

trials.full_entry: LOVIT (Lamontagne et al., 2022): In septic patients in the ICU, intravenous vitamin C was associated with a higher risk of death or persistent organ dysfunction.
trials.description: In septic patients in the ICU, intravenous vitamin C was associated with a higher risk of death or persistent organ dysfunction.

trials.full_entry: MARIK (Marik et al., 2017): A highly controversial, single-center, before-and-after study suggesting a dramatic mortality benefit from hydrocortisone, ascorbic acid, and thiamine ("HAT" therapy) that has not been replicated in RCTs.
trials.description: A highly controversial, single-center, before-and-after study suggesting a dramatic mortality benefit from hydrocortisone, ascorbic acid, and thiamine ("HAT" therapy) that has not been replicated in RCTs.

trials.full_entry: PROWESS (Bernard et al., 2001): Initially showed a mortality benefit for drotrecogin alfa (activated protein C), but the drug was later withdrawn after subsequent trials failed to confirm a benefit.
trials.description: Initially showed a mortality benefit for drotrecogin alfa (activated protein C), but the drug was later withdrawn after subsequent trials failed to confirm a benefit.

trials.full_entry: STASH (2023): Investigated the effect of early statin therapy on organ dysfunction in adult patients with sepsis.
trials.description: Investigated the effect of early statin therapy on organ dysfunction in adult patients with sepsis.

trials.full_entry: VISEP (The VISEP study group, 2009): Found that tight glycemic control led to more severe hypoglycemia without a survival benefit in septic patients.
trials.description: Found that tight glycemic control led to more severe hypoglycemia without a survival benefit in septic patients.

trials.full_entry: ARDSNet (ARMA) (The ARDS Network, 2000): The definitive trial establishing that low tidal volume ventilation (6 ml/kg predicted body weight) significantly reduces mortality compared to traditional high volumes (12 ml/kg) in ARDS.
trials.description: The definitive trial establishing that low tidal volume ventilation (6 ml/kg predicted body weight) significantly reduces mortality compared to traditional high volumes (12 ml/kg) in ARDS.

trials.full_entry: PermiT (Murthy et al., 2014): Showed that permissive hypercapnia was safe and did not worsen outcomes in patients with moderate-to-severe ARDS.
trials.description: Showed that permissive hypercapnia was safe and did not worsen outcomes in patients with moderate-to-severe ARDS.

trials.full_entry: ALVEOLI (2004), ExPress (2008), LOV-S (2008): A series of trials comparing higher vs. lower PEEP strategies in ARDS which, collectively, showed no overall mortality benefit for a high PEEP strategy.
trials.description: A series of trials comparing higher vs. lower PEEP strategies in ARDS which, collectively, showed no overall mortality benefit for a high PEEP strategy.

trials.full_entry: ART (The ART Investigators, 2017): Found that a strategy of aggressive recruitment maneuvers followed by PEEP titration resulted in increased mortality.
trials.description: Found that a strategy of aggressive recruitment maneuvers followed by PEEP titration resulted in increased mortality.

trials.full_entry: NICO (EPVent-2) (2017): In patients with moderate-to-severe ARDS, esophageal pressure-guided PEEP titration did not significantly improve outcomes compared to an empirical high PEEP strategy.
trials.description: In patients with moderate-to-severe ARDS, esophageal pressure-guided PEEP titration did not significantly improve outcomes compared to an empirical high PEEP strategy.

trials.full_entry: PROSEVA (Guérin et al., 2013): A landmark trial demonstrating a profound mortality benefit for early and prolonged prone positioning in patients with severe ARDS.
trials.description: A landmark trial demonstrating a profound mortality benefit for early and prolonged prone positioning in patients with severe ARDS.

trials.full_entry: PROTRATA (2021): A trial focused on the application and outcomes of prone positioning for trauma patients who developed ARDS.
trials.description: A trial focused on the application and outcomes of prone positioning for trauma patients who developed ARDS.

trials.full_entry: REST (2013): The first of two major prone positioning trials that did not show a mortality benefit, setting the stage for PROSEVA.
trials.description: The first of two major prone positioning trials that did not show a mortality benefit, setting the stage for PROSEVA.

trials.full_entry: ACURASYS (Papazian et al., 2010): Showed that a 48-hour infusion of cisatracurium in early, severe ARDS improved 90-day mortality.
trials.description: Showed that a 48-hour infusion of cisatracurium in early, severe ARDS improved 90-day mortality.

trials.full_entry: ROSE (The PETAL Clinical Trials Network, 2019): A subsequent, larger trial that found no mortality benefit with early neuromuscular blockade in moderate-to-severe ARDS.
trials.description: A subsequent, larger trial that found no mortality benefit with early neuromuscular blockade in moderate-to-severe ARDS.

trials.full_entry: CESAR (Peek et al., 2009): Showed that referral to a specialized ECMO center improved survival without severe disability for patients with severe respiratory failure.
trials.description: Showed that referral to a specialized ECMO center improved survival without severe disability for patients with severe respiratory failure.

trials.full_entry: EOLIA (Combes et al., 2018): In patients with very severe ARDS, early veno- venous ECMO did not result in a significantly lower 60-day mortality, but was likely underpowered.
trials.description: In patients with very severe ARDS, early veno- venous ECMO did not result in a significantly lower 60-day mortality, but was likely underpowered.

trials.full_entry: OSCAR (Young et al., 2013) & OSCILLATE (Ferguson et al., 2013): Two concurrent trials that definitively showed High-Frequency Oscillatory Ventilation (HFOV) was not beneficial and was potentially harmful compared to conventional ventilation for ARDS.
trials.description: Two concurrent trials that definitively showed High-Frequency Oscillatory Ventilation (HFOV) was not beneficial and was potentially harmful compared to conventional ventilation for ARDS.

trials.full_entry: Brochard et al. (1995): A landmark trial demonstrating the efficacy of non-invasive ventilation (NIV) for acute exacerbations of COPD. (Also known as the trial by YANG).
trials.description: A landmark trial demonstrating the efficacy of non-invasive ventilation (NIV) for acute exacerbations of COPD. (Also known as the trial by YANG).

trials.full_entry: CHOICE (2022): Compared high-flow nasal cannula with conventional oxygen therapy for preventing reintubation in high-risk patients.
trials.description: Compared high-flow nasal cannula with conventional oxygen therapy for preventing reintubation in high-risk patients.

trials.full_entry: DELCLAUX (Delclaux et al., 2000): In selected patients with postextubation respiratory failure, noninvasive ventilation reduces reintubation rates and ICU mortality.
trials.description: In selected patients with postextubation respiratory failure, noninvasive ventilation reduces reintubation rates and ICU mortality.

trials.full_entry: ESCORSELL (Hernández et al., 2016): Showed that noninvasive ventilation (NIV) did not reduce the rate of reintubation after cardiothoracic surgery compared to high- flow nasal cannula (HFNC).
trials.description: Showed that noninvasive ventilation (NIV) did not reduce the rate of reintubation after cardiothoracic surgery compared to high- flow nasal cannula (HFNC).

trials.full_entry: Esteban et al (2004): A major observational study describing weaning practices and outcomes from mechanical ventilation across multiple countries.
trials.description: A major observational study describing weaning practices and outcomes from mechanical ventilation across multiple countries.

trials.full_entry: FLORALI (Frat et al., 2015): In acute hypoxemic respiratory failure, high-flow nasal cannula was associated with lower mortality compared to standard oxygen or NIV.
trials.description: In acute hypoxemic respiratory failure, high-flow nasal cannula was associated with lower mortality compared to standard oxygen or NIV.

trials.full_entry: HOT-ICU (Schjørring et al., 2021): Compared a lower versus higher oxygenation target (PaO2 of 60 vs 90 mmHg) in ICU patients with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure.
trials.description: Compared a lower versus higher oxygenation target (PaO2 of 60 vs 90 mmHg) in ICU patients with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure.

trials.full_entry: ICU-ROX (The ICU-ROX Investigators, 2021): In ventilated ICU patients, a conservative oxygen strategy (targeting SpO₂ 90-96%) did not significantly alter outcomes compared to a liberal strategy.
trials.description: In ventilated ICU patients, a conservative oxygen strategy (targeting SpO₂ 90-96%) did not significantly alter outcomes compared to a liberal strategy.

trials.full_entry: SUPERNOVA (2021): Compared helmet noninvasive ventilation with face mask NIV for acute hypoxemic respiratory failure.
trials.description: Compared helmet noninvasive ventilation with face mask NIV for acute hypoxemic respiratory failure.

trials.full_entry: SUBIRÀ (Hernández et al., 2019): In ICU patients at high risk of reintubation, HFNC was superior to conventional oxygen therapy for preventing reintubation.
trials.description: In ICU patients at high risk of reintubation, HFNC was superior to conventional oxygen therapy for preventing reintubation.

trials.full_entry: TracMan (The TracMan Collaboration, 2013): Early tracheostomy (within 4 days) did not confer a mortality benefit over late tracheostomy (after 10 days).
trials.description: Early tracheostomy (within 4 days) did not confer a mortality benefit over late tracheostomy (after 10 days).

trials.full_entry: WIND (Subirà et al., 2022): A strategy of progressively reducing Pressure Support Ventilation (PSV) resulted in more ventilator-free days compared to a daily short Spontaneous Breathing Trial (SBT).
trials.description: A strategy of progressively reducing Pressure Support Ventilation (PSV) resulted in more ventilator-free days compared to a daily short Spontaneous Breathing Trial (SBT).

trials.full_entry: YANG (1991): A seminal paper demonstrating the efficacy of non-invasive ventilation (NIV) for preventing intubation in patients with acute exacerbations of COPD. (Also known as the trial by Brochard et al., 1995).
trials.author_year: (Also known as the trial by Brochard et al., 1995)
trials.description: A seminal paper demonstrating the efficacy of non-invasive ventilation (NIV) for preventing intubation in patients with acute exacerbations of COPD. (Also known as the trial by Brochard et al., 1995).

trials.full_entry: DEXA-ARDS (Villar et al., 2020): In moderate-to-severe ARDS, treatment with dexamethasone reduced the duration of mechanical ventilation and lowered mortality.
trials.description: In moderate-to-severe ARDS, treatment with dexamethasone reduced the duration of mechanical ventilation and lowered mortality.

trials.full_entry: FACTT (The ARDS Clinical Trials Network, 2006): A conservative (diuretic- based) fluid management strategy in ARDS improved lung function and shortened ventilator duration.
trials.description: A conservative (diuretic- based) fluid management strategy in ARDS improved lung function and shortened ventilator duration.

trials.full_entry: FIRST-ABC (2023): Investigated furosemide versus spironolactone in patients with ARDS.
trials.description: Investigated furosemide versus spironolactone in patients with ARDS.

trials.full_entry: MEDURI (Meduri et al., 2007, updated 2018): Showed that prolonged administration of methylprednisolone in patients with unresolving ARDS improved lung function and reduced mortality.
trials.description: Showed that prolonged administration of methylprednisolone in patients with unresolving ARDS improved lung function and reduced mortality.

trials.full_entry: BOUGIE (The BOUGIE Trial Investigators, 2022): In critically ill adults, the use of a tracheal tube introducer (bougie) did not significantly increase first-pass intubation success compared with a stylet.
trials.description: In critically ill adults, the use of a tracheal tube introducer (bougie) did not significantly increase first-pass intubation success compared with a stylet.

trials.full_entry: DEVICE (The DEVICE Trial Investigators, 2023): In critically ill adults, video laryngoscopy resulted in a higher first-pass intubation success rate compared to direct laryngoscopy.
trials.description: In critically ill adults, video laryngoscopy resulted in a higher first-pass intubation success rate compared to direct laryngoscopy.

trials.full_entry: LAU (2000): Investigated the use and safety of the Laryngeal Mask Airway (LMA) as a primary airway device in unconscious patients.
trials.description: Investigated the use and safety of the Laryngeal Mask Airway (LMA) as a primary airway device in unconscious patients.

trials.full_entry: PREPARE II (The PREPARE II Investigators, 2023): Found that an intravenous fluid bolus given before tracheal intubation in critically ill adults did not improve outcomes and may cause harm.
trials.description: Found that an intravenous fluid bolus given before tracheal intubation in critically ill adults did not improve outcomes and may cause harm.

description: This section covers management of acute brain and spinal cord injuries, including TBI,

trials.full_entry: Besson et al. (1995): An early trial in head-injured patients suggesting benefit from early enteral nutrition.
trials.description: An early trial in head-injured patients suggesting benefit from early enteral nutrition.

trials.full_entry: CRASH (The CRASH trial collaborators, 2004): A large trial that showed corticosteroids given for TBI increased the risk of death, leading to their contraindication.
trials.description: A large trial that showed corticosteroids given for TBI increased the risk of death, leading to their contraindication.

trials.full_entry: CRASH-3 (The CRASH-3 trial collaborators, 2019): Showed that TXA safely reduces head-injury-related death in patients with mild-to-moderate TBI.
trials.description: Showed that TXA safely reduces head-injury-related death in patients with mild-to-moderate TBI.

trials.full_entry: DECRA (Cooper et al., 2011): In severe, diffuse TBI, early decompressive craniectomy improved ICP control but led to worse functional outcomes.
trials.description: In severe, diffuse TBI, early decompressive craniectomy improved ICP control but led to worse functional outcomes.

trials.full_entry: EPO-TBI (The EPO-TBI Investigators, 2019): Erythropoietin did not improve neurologic outcomes at 6 months in patients with moderate or severe TBI.
trials.description: Erythropoietin did not improve neurologic outcomes at 6 months in patients with moderate or severe TBI.

trials.full_entry: EUROHYP-1 (2018): A trial of therapeutic hypothermia in patients with severe traumatic brain injury that was stopped early.
trials.description: A trial of therapeutic hypothermia in patients with severe traumatic brain injury that was stopped early.

trials.full_entry: Eurotherm3235 (Andrews et al., 2015): Therapeutic hypothermia did not improve outcomes after TBI and may have been harmful.
trials.description: Therapeutic hypothermia did not improve outcomes after TBI and may have been harmful.

trials.full_entry: PICKARD (Juul et al., 1989): A foundational study showing that mannitol could effectively reduce intracranial pressure (ICP).
trials.description: A foundational study showing that mannitol could effectively reduce intracranial pressure (ICP).

trials.full_entry: RESCUEicp (Hutchinson et al., 2016): In TBI with refractory ICP, decompressive craniectomy lowered mortality but was associated with higher rates of vegetative state or severe disability.
trials.description: In TBI with refractory ICP, decompressive craniectomy lowered mortality but was associated with higher rates of vegetative state or severe disability.

trials.full_entry: TEMKIN (Temkin et al., 1990): Showed that prophylactic phenytoin was effective for preventing early post-traumatic seizures, but not late seizures.
trials.description: Showed that prophylactic phenytoin was effective for preventing early post-traumatic seizures, but not late seizures.

trials.full_entry: BRACKEN I, II, & III (NASCIS Trials) (Bracken et al.): A controversial series of trials suggesting a benefit for high-dose methylprednisolone if given within 8 hours of acute spinal cord injury.
trials.description: A controversial series of trials suggesting a benefit for high-dose methylprednisolone if given within 8 hours of acute spinal cord injury.

trials.full_entry: DAWN (Nogueira et al., 2018) & DEFUSE 3 (Albers et al., 2018): Landmark trials that showed a dramatic benefit for mechanical thrombectomy in select stroke patients up to 24 hours after onset.
trials.description: Landmark trials that showed a dramatic benefit for mechanical thrombectomy in select stroke patients up to 24 hours after onset.

trials.full_entry: DESTINY (Jüttler et al., 2007): Demonstrated that early decompressive hemicraniectomy improved outcomes in malignant middle cerebral artery infarction.
trials.description: Demonstrated that early decompressive hemicraniectomy improved outcomes in malignant middle cerebral artery infarction.

trials.full_entry: DESTINY II (Jüttler et al., 2014): Extended the findings of DESTINY to patients over 60 years of age.
trials.description: Extended the findings of DESTINY to patients over 60 years of age.

trials.full_entry: ECLASS III (2008): A likely typo for the ECASS III trial, which extended the time window for IV tPA to 4.5 hours for select stroke patients.
trials.description: A likely typo for the ECASS III trial, which extended the time window for IV tPA to 4.5 hours for select stroke patients.

trials.full_entry: ESCAPE (Goyal et al., 2015): A pivotal trial showing a strong benefit for endovascular thrombectomy in acute ischemic stroke.
trials.description: A pivotal trial showing a strong benefit for endovascular thrombectomy in acute ischemic stroke.

trials.full_entry: HAMLET (Hofmeijer et al., 2011): A trial on hemicraniectomy for space-occupying middle cerebral artery infarction.
trials.description: A trial on hemicraniectomy for space-occupying middle cerebral artery infarction.

trials.full_entry: IST-3 (The IST-3 collaborative group, 2012): A large pragmatic trial that assessed the benefits and risks of intravenous thrombolysis for acute ischemic stroke up to 6 hours.
trials.description: A large pragmatic trial that assessed the benefits and risks of intravenous thrombolysis for acute ischemic stroke up to 6 hours.

trials.full_entry: NINDS (The NINDS rt-PA Stroke Study Group, 1995): The pivotal trial establishing that IV tPA within 3 hours of symptom onset improved outcomes in acute ischemic stroke.
trials.description: The pivotal trial establishing that IV tPA within 3 hours of symptom onset improved outcomes in acute ischemic stroke.

trials.full_entry: SETPOINT2 (2022): Compared early (within 5 days) versus standard (after 10 days) tracheostomy in patients with severe stroke requiring mechanical ventilation.
trials.description: Compared early (within 5 days) versus standard (after 10 days) tracheostomy in patients with severe stroke requiring mechanical ventilation.

trials.full_entry: STICH (Mendelow et al., 2005) & STICH II (Mendelow et al., 2013): Found that early surgery for intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) did not have a clear overall benefit, but may help in superficial lobar hematomas.
trials.description: Found that early surgery for intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) did not have a clear overall benefit, but may help in superficial lobar hematomas.

trials.full_entry: TICH-2 (The TICH-2 Investigators, 2018): Tranexamic acid (TXA) for acute ICH did not improve functional outcomes at 90 days.
trials.description: Tranexamic acid (TXA) for acute ICH did not improve functional outcomes at 90 days.

trials.full_entry: DE GANS (de Gans et al., 2002): A landmark trial showing that adjunctive dexamethasone improved outcomes and reduced mortality in adults with acute bacterial meningitis.
trials.description: A landmark trial showing that adjunctive dexamethasone improved outcomes and reduced mortality in adults with acute bacterial meningitis.

trials.full_entry: DRAKULOVIC (1999): Investigated the use of dexamethasone in treating neonatal bacterial meningitis.
trials.description: Investigated the use of dexamethasone in treating neonatal bacterial meningitis.

trials.full_entry: ISAT (Molyneux et al., 2002): In ruptured cerebral aneurysms, endovascular coiling was associated with better disability-free survival at 1 year compared to surgical clipping.
trials.description: In ruptured cerebral aneurysms, endovascular coiling was associated with better disability-free survival at 1 year compared to surgical clipping.

trials.full_entry: Magpie (The Magpie Trial Collaborative Group, 2002): In women with pre- eclampsia, magnesium sulfate halved the risk of eclampsia and reduced maternal mortality.
trials.description: In women with pre- eclampsia, magnesium sulfate halved the risk of eclampsia and reduced maternal mortality.

trials.full_entry: Bernard et al. (2002) & HACA (The HACA Study Group, 2002): Two foundational trials demonstrating that therapeutic hypothermia (32-34°C) improved neurological outcomes after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA).
trials.description: Two foundational trials demonstrating that therapeutic hypothermia (32-34°C) improved neurological outcomes after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA).

trials.full_entry: BOX (Kjaergaard et al., 2022): In comatose OHCA survivors, targeting a lower blood pressure or a restrictive oxygen target did not improve outcomes compared to higher targets.
trials.description: In comatose OHCA survivors, targeting a lower blood pressure or a restrictive oxygen target did not improve outcomes compared to higher targets.

trials.full_entry: HYPERION (Lascarrou et al., 2019): In patients with non-shockable rhythm OHCA, moderate therapeutic hypothermia (33°C) resulted in a better neurologic outcome compared to targeted normothermia (37°C).
trials.description: In patients with non-shockable rhythm OHCA, moderate therapeutic hypothermia (33°C) resulted in a better neurologic outcome compared to targeted normothermia (37°C).

trials.full_entry: PRACTICE (2024): Routine administration of a P2Y12 inhibitor after OHCA did not improve survival with a favorable neurologic outcome.
trials.description: Routine administration of a P2Y12 inhibitor after OHCA did not improve survival with a favorable neurologic outcome.

trials.full_entry: TAME (2022): Compared targeted temperature management at 33°C with targeted normothermia after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest.
trials.description: Compared targeted temperature management at 33°C with targeted normothermia after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest.

trials.full_entry: TROICA (Kudenchuk et al., 2008): Investigated the use of amiodarone during resuscitation for out-of-hospital cardiac arrest with refractory ventricular fibrillation.
trials.description: Investigated the use of amiodarone during resuscitation for out-of-hospital cardiac arrest with refractory ventricular fibrillation.

trials.full_entry: TTM (Nielsen et al., 2013): A pivotal trial that found no difference between targeting a temperature of 33°C versus 36°C in comatose survivors of OHCA.
trials.description: A pivotal trial that found no difference between targeting a temperature of 33°C versus 36°C in comatose survivors of OHCA.

trials.full_entry: TTM 48 (2013): A sub-study of the TTM trial which found that extending targeted temperature management to 48 hours did not improve neurologic outcomes.
trials.description: A sub-study of the TTM trial which found that extending targeted temperature management to 48 hours did not improve neurologic outcomes.

trials.full_entry: TTM2 (Dankiewicz et al., 2021): Compared targeted hypothermia (33°C) with targeted normothermia while avoiding fever, finding that hypothermia did not reduce mortality and may have increased arrhythmias.
trials.description: Compared targeted hypothermia (33°C) with targeted normothermia while avoiding fever, finding that hypothermia did not reduce mortality and may have increased arrhythmias.

trials.full_entry: VAM-IHCA (The VAM-IHCA trial group, 2024): Vasopressin-epinephrine- methylprednisolone during in-hospital cardiac arrest did not improve survival to discharge with good neurologic outcome.
trials.description: Vasopressin-epinephrine- methylprednisolone during in-hospital cardiac arrest did not improve survival to discharge with good neurologic outcome.

trials.full_entry: 3Mg (Gillinov et al., 2013): In patients undergoing cardiac surgery, IV magnesium did not reduce the incidence of postoperative atrial fibrillation.
trials.description: In patients undergoing cardiac surgery, IV magnesium did not reduce the incidence of postoperative atrial fibrillation.

trials.full_entry: CARRESS-HF (Bart et al., 2012): Compared ultrafiltration to stepped pharmacologic therapy for patients with acute decompensated heart failure and cardiorenal syndrome.
trials.description: Compared ultrafiltration to stepped pharmacologic therapy for patients with acute decompensated heart failure and cardiorenal syndrome.

trials.full_entry: CHASTER (The CHASTER study group, 2022): In patients with vasoplegic shock after cardiac surgery, hydrocortisone did not significantly reduce the duration of vasopressor therapy.
trials.description: In patients with vasoplegic shock after cardiac surgery, hydrocortisone did not significantly reduce the duration of vasopressor therapy.

trials.full_entry: COMMIT (The COMMIT collaborative group, 2005): Landmark cardiology trial establishing the benefit of beta-blockers in acute MI.
trials.description: Landmark cardiology trial establishing the benefit of beta-blockers in acute MI.

trials.full_entry: CULPRIT-SHOCK (Thiele et al., 2017): In cardiogenic shock, immediate PCI of only the culprit lesion was superior to immediate multi-vessel PCI.
trials.description: In cardiogenic shock, immediate PCI of only the culprit lesion was superior to immediate multi-vessel PCI.

trials.full_entry: DANAMI-2 (Andersen et al., 2003): Showed that transferring patients with STEMI to an invasive center for primary PCI was superior to on-site fibrinolysis.
trials.description: Showed that transferring patients with STEMI to an invasive center for primary PCI was superior to on-site fibrinolysis.

trials.full_entry: DIABOLO (2018): Investigated the use of methylene blue for vasoplegic syndrome after cardiac surgery.
trials.description: Investigated the use of methylene blue for vasoplegic syndrome after cardiac surgery.

trials.full_entry: ECLS-SHOCK (The ECLS-SHOCK trial group, 2023): Early extracorporeal life support (ECLS/ECMO) did not reduce mortality in patients with severe cardiogenic shock from MI.
trials.description: Early extracorporeal life support (ECLS/ECMO) did not reduce mortality in patients with severe cardiogenic shock from MI.

trials.full_entry: IABP-SHOCK II (Thiele et al., 2012): The intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) did not reduce mortality in patients with cardiogenic shock complicating MI.
trials.description: The intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) did not reduce mortality in patients with cardiogenic shock complicating MI.

trials.full_entry: ISIS-2 (The ISIS-2 Collaborative Group, 1988): Landmark cardiology trial establishing the benefit of aspirin in acute MI.
trials.description: Landmark cardiology trial establishing the benefit of aspirin in acute MI.

trials.full_entry: NORDISTEMI (2023): Compared an immediate versus a delayed invasive strategy in patients with non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction.
trials.description: Compared an immediate versus a delayed invasive strategy in patients with non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction.

trials.full_entry: REMATCH (Rose et al., 2001): A pivotal trial showing that left ventricular assist devices (LVADs) improved survival in end-stage heart failure patients ineligible for transplant.
trials.description: A pivotal trial showing that left ventricular assist devices (LVADs) improved survival in end-stage heart failure patients ineligible for transplant.

trials.full_entry: SHOCK (Hochman et al., 1999): In patients with cardiogenic shock post-MI, early revascularization reduced mortality compared to initial medical stabilization.
trials.description: In patients with cardiogenic shock post-MI, early revascularization reduced mortality compared to initial medical stabilization.

trials.full_entry: UPLIFT (2007): A major trial on the long-term effects of the anticholinergic drug tiotropium in patients with COPD.
trials.description: A major trial on the long-term effects of the anticholinergic drug tiotropium in patients with COPD.

trials.full_entry: CRASH-2 (The CRASH-2 trial collaborators, 2010): Landmark trial showing that early TXA (within 3 hours) reduces all-cause mortality in bleeding trauma patients.
trials.description: Landmark trial showing that early TXA (within 3 hours) reduces all-cause mortality in bleeding trauma patients.

trials.full_entry: CRYOSTAT-2 (The CRYOSTAT-2 trial group, 2023): Found that early administration of cryoprecipitate to trauma patients with major hemorrhage did not improve survival.
trials.description: Found that early administration of cryoprecipitate to trauma patients with major hemorrhage did not improve survival.

trials.full_entry: HALT-IT (The HALT-IT Trial Collaborators, 2020): In patients with acute gastrointestinal bleeding, TXA did not reduce death from hemorrhage.
trials.description: In patients with acute gastrointestinal bleeding, TXA did not reduce death from hemorrhage.

trials.full_entry: ITACTIC (2018): Investigated a transfusion strategy guided by viscoelastic hemostatic assays versus conventional coagulation tests in trauma.
trials.description: Investigated a transfusion strategy guided by viscoelastic hemostatic assays versus conventional coagulation tests in trauma.

trials.full_entry: PATCH-Trauma (The PATCH-Trauma Investigators, 2023): Found that early administration of platelets to trauma patients with major hemorrhage did not improve survival.
trials.description: Found that early administration of platelets to trauma patients with major hemorrhage did not improve survival.

trials.full_entry: PROPPR (Holcomb et al., 2015): In massive hemorrhage, resuscitation with plasma, platelets, and RBCs in a 1:1:1 ratio improved hemostasis compared to a 1:1:2 ratio.
trials.description: In massive hemorrhage, resuscitation with plasma, platelets, and RBCs in a 1:1:1 ratio improved hemostasis compared to a 1:1:2 ratio.

trials.full_entry: RePHILL (The RePHILL trial collaborators, 2023): In prehospital trauma resuscitation, blood products were not superior to saline.
trials.description: In prehospital trauma resuscitation, blood products were not superior to saline.

trials.full_entry: WOMAN (The WOMAN Trial Collaborators, 2017): TXA reduces death due to bleeding in women with post-partum hemorrhage.
trials.description: TXA reduces death due to bleeding in women with post-partum hemorrhage.

trials.full_entry: IMPROVE (2010): A study on the prevention of venous thromboembolism in medically ill patients.
trials.description: A study on the prevention of venous thromboembolism in medically ill patients.

trials.full_entry: MAPPET-3 (2002): A trial that studied the efficacy and safety of thrombolysis in patients with intermediate-risk pulmonary embolism.
trials.description: A trial that studied the efficacy and safety of thrombolysis in patients with intermediate-risk pulmonary embolism.

trials.full_entry: MOPETT (2013): Investigated a "safe dose" of thrombolysis for patients with moderate pulmonary embolism.
trials.description: Investigated a "safe dose" of thrombolysis for patients with moderate pulmonary embolism.

trials.full_entry: PEERLESS (Ongoing): A trial comparing catheter-directed thrombolysis versus anticoagulation alone for patients with submassive pulmonary embolism.
trials.description: A trial comparing catheter-directed thrombolysis versus anticoagulation alone for patients with submassive pulmonary embolism.

trials.full_entry: PEITHO (Meyer et al., 2014): In patients with submassive PE, thrombolysis prevented hemodynamic decompensation but significantly increased the risk of major hemorrhage.
trials.description: In patients with submassive PE, thrombolysis prevented hemodynamic decompensation but significantly increased the risk of major hemorrhage.

trials.full_entry: POISE (2008): A large trial that found perioperative beta-blockade reduced MI but increased mortality and stroke in non-cardiac surgery.
trials.description: A large trial that found perioperative beta-blockade reduced MI but increased mortality and stroke in non-cardiac surgery.

trials.full_entry: PREVENT (The PREVENT trial group, 2016): In ICU patients, low-dose unfractionated heparin for VTE prophylaxis did not reduce the rate of death from any cause at 90 days.
trials.description: In ICU patients, low-dose unfractionated heparin for VTE prophylaxis did not reduce the rate of death from any cause at 90 days.

trials.full_entry: PROTECT (Cook et al., 2011): Compared dalteparin to unfractionated heparin for VTE prophylaxis; dalteparin was superior in reducing rates of pulmonary embolism.
trials.description: Compared dalteparin to unfractionated heparin for VTE prophylaxis; dalteparin was superior in reducing rates of pulmonary embolism.

trials.full_entry: AID-ICU (The AID-ICU trial group, 2022): Haloperidol had no significant effect on days alive and out of the hospital for ICU patients with delirium.
trials.description: Haloperidol had no significant effect on days alive and out of the hospital for ICU patients with delirium.

trials.full_entry: COIITSS (Mirzanejad et al., 2012): Clonidine was not significantly better than placebo at facilitating weaning from sedation and mechanical ventilation.
trials.description: Clonidine was not significantly better than placebo at facilitating weaning from sedation and mechanical ventilation.

trials.full_entry: DECIMAL (2010): A trial comparing dexmedetomidine versus midazolam for sedation in the ICU.
trials.description: A trial comparing dexmedetomidine versus midazolam for sedation in the ICU.

trials.full_entry: DEVLIN et al. (Devlin et al.): Not an intervention trial, but foundational work in developing and validating delirium assessment tools like the CAM-ICU.
trials.description: Not an intervention trial, but foundational work in developing and validating delirium assessment tools like the CAM-ICU.

trials.full_entry: Kress et al. (2000) (ABC Trial): Showed that daily interruption of sedative infusions ("sedation holiday") decreased the duration of mechanical ventilation.
trials.description: Showed that daily interruption of sedative infusions ("sedation holiday") decreased the duration of mechanical ventilation.

trials.full_entry: MENDS (Pandharipande et al., 2008): In septic patients, dexmedetomidine resulted in more days alive without delirium or coma compared to lorazepam.
trials.description: In septic patients, dexmedetomidine resulted in more days alive without delirium or coma compared to lorazepam.

trials.full_entry: MENDS2 (Hughes et al., 2018): In septic patients with acute respiratory failure, there was no significant difference between dexmedetomidine and propofol.
trials.description: In septic patients with acute respiratory failure, there was no significant difference between dexmedetomidine and propofol.

trials.full_entry: MIND-USA (Girard et al., 2013): Haloperidol and ziprasidone did not alter the duration of delirium in ICU patients.
trials.description: Haloperidol and ziprasidone did not alter the duration of delirium in ICU patients.

trials.full_entry: NONSEDA (The NONSEDA trial group, 2020): A strategy of no sedation versus light sedation resulted in more ventilator-free days.
trials.description: A strategy of no sedation versus light sedation resulted in more ventilator-free days.

trials.full_entry: PRODEX (2012): A trial comparing dexmedetomidine versus propofol for light to moderate sedation in ventilated patients.
trials.description: A trial comparing dexmedetomidine versus propofol for light to moderate sedation in ventilated patients.

trials.full_entry: SEDCOM (Riker et al., 2009): Dexmedetomidine reduced the duration of mechanical ventilation and the prevalence of coma compared to midazolam.
trials.description: Dexmedetomidine reduced the duration of mechanical ventilation and the prevalence of coma compared to midazolam.

trials.full_entry: SLEAP (2012): A trial comparing protocolized sedation and weaning strategies versus usual care.
trials.description: A trial comparing protocolized sedation and weaning strategies versus usual care.

trials.full_entry: AKIKI (Gaudry et al., 2016): A multicenter trial which found a delayed strategy for initiating RRT was not inferior to an early strategy.
trials.description: A multicenter trial which found a delayed strategy for initiating RRT was not inferior to an early strategy.

trials.full_entry: AKIKI2 (Gaudry et al., 2021): In patients with severe AKI and sepsis who were oliguric for >72 hours, a strategy of "more-delayed" RRT initiation increased mortality.
trials.description: In patients with severe AKI and sepsis who were oliguric for >72 hours, a strategy of "more-delayed" RRT initiation increased mortality.

trials.full_entry: ATN (The VA/NIH ARF Trial Network, 2008) & RENAL (The RENAL Study Investigators, 2009): Two large trials that demonstrated no survival benefit with higher intensity RRT compared to standard, less-intensive RRT.
trials.description: Two large trials that demonstrated no survival benefit with higher intensity RRT compared to standard, less-intensive RRT.

trials.full_entry: Bouchard (2005): An important observational study identifying risk factors for acute kidney injury (AKI) in the ICU and confirming its strong association with mortality.
trials.description: An important observational study identifying risk factors for acute kidney injury (AKI) in the ICU and confirming its strong association with mortality.

trials.full_entry: ELAIN (Zarbock et al., 2016): A single-center trial that found early initiation of RRT reduced mortality.
trials.description: A single-center trial that found early initiation of RRT reduced mortality.

trials.full_entry: STARRT-AKI (The STARRT-AKI Investigators, 2020): The largest trial on the topic, which found that an accelerated RRT initiation strategy did not reduce mortality compared to a standard (delayed) strategy.
trials.description: The largest trial on the topic, which found that an accelerated RRT initiation strategy did not reduce mortality compared to a standard (delayed) strategy.

trials.full_entry: ULTRA (2016): Compared the outcomes of early versus late initiation of renal replacement therapy (RRT) in patients with acute kidney injury.
trials.description: Compared the outcomes of early versus late initiation of renal replacement therapy (RRT) in patients with acute kidney injury.

trials.full_entry: ZARBOCK (2020) (PREV-AKI): Remote ischemic preconditioning did not prevent AKI in high-risk patients undergoing cardiac surgery.
trials.description: Remote ischemic preconditioning did not prevent AKI in high-risk patients undergoing cardiac surgery.

trials.full_entry: LEUVEN I & II (van den Berghe et al., 2001, 2006): The original trials on tight glycemic control, which showed benefit in a surgical ICU but not a medical ICU.
trials.description: The original trials on tight glycemic control, which showed benefit in a surgical ICU but not a medical ICU.

trials.full_entry: NICE-SUGAR (The NICE-SUGAR Study Investigators, 2009): The definitive trial showing tight glycemic control (target 81-108 mg/dL) increased mortality compared to a conventional approach (<180 mg/dL).
trials.description: The definitive trial showing tight glycemic control (target 81-108 mg/dL) increased mortality compared to a conventional approach (<180 mg/dL).

trials.full_entry: Andriulli et al. (2008): Studied high-dose vs. low-dose proton pump inhibitors after endoscopic hemostasis for peptic ulcer bleeding.
trials.description: Studied high-dose vs. low-dose proton pump inhibitors after endoscopic hemostasis for peptic ulcer bleeding.

trials.full_entry: CALORIES (The CALORIES Trial Investigators, 2014): Compared early parenteral nutrition vs. enteral nutrition in critically ill adults and found no difference in mortality.
trials.description: Compared early parenteral nutrition vs. enteral nutrition in critically ill adults and found no difference in mortality.

trials.full_entry: EDEN (The ARDS Network, 2012): In patients with ARDS, trophic (minimal) enteral feeding for the first 6 days was not different from full enteral feeding.
trials.description: In patients with ARDS, trophic (minimal) enteral feeding for the first 6 days was not different from full enteral feeding.

trials.full_entry: EPaNIC (Casaer et al., 2011): Showed that withholding parenteral nutrition for a week ("late PN") was superior to early PN when enteral feeding was not feasible.
trials.description: Showed that withholding parenteral nutrition for a week ("late PN") was superior to early PN when enteral feeding was not feasible.

trials.full_entry: NUTRIREA-3 (The NUTRIREA-3 study group, 2022): Early, low-dose parenteral nutrition added to enteral nutrition in shocked patients did not significantly change mortality.
trials.description: Early, low-dose parenteral nutrition added to enteral nutrition in shocked patients did not significantly change mortality.

trials.full_entry: REDOXS (Heyland et al., 2013): Showed that glutamine and antioxidant supplementation provided no benefit and may cause harm in critically ill patients.
trials.description: Showed that glutamine and antioxidant supplementation provided no benefit and may cause harm in critically ill patients.

trials.full_entry: REVISE (2024): Pantoprazole significantly reduced GI bleeding but was associated with a small but statistically significant increase in 90-day mortality.
trials.description: Pantoprazole significantly reduced GI bleeding but was associated with a small but statistically significant increase in 90-day mortality.

trials.full_entry: SUP-ICU (Krag et al., 2018): Pantoprazole for stress ulcer prophylaxis lowered GI bleeding rates but did not affect mortality.
trials.description: Pantoprazole for stress ulcer prophylaxis lowered GI bleeding rates but did not affect mortality.

trials.full_entry: TELSTAR (2023): Studied the use of low-dose alteplase and DNase for treating pleural infection.
trials.description: Studied the use of low-dose alteplase and DNase for treating pleural infection.

trials.full_entry: MINT (2021): Compared a restrictive versus liberal transfusion strategy in patients with acute myocardial infarction and anemia.
trials.description: Compared a restrictive versus liberal transfusion strategy in patients with acute myocardial infarction and anemia.

trials.full_entry: SORT (1999) & TRICC (1999): Seminal trials showing a restrictive transfusion strategy (trigger Hb <7 g/dL) was at least as effective as and possibly superior to a liberal strategy (trigger <10 g/dL) in general ICU patients.
trials.description: Seminal trials showing a restrictive transfusion strategy (trigger Hb <7 g/dL) was at least as effective as and possibly superior to a liberal strategy (trigger <10 g/dL) in general ICU patients.

trials.full_entry: TRICS-III (2017): A large trial that confirmed a restrictive transfusion strategy is non-inferior to a liberal one in patients undergoing cardiac surgery.
trials.description: A large trial that confirmed a restrictive transfusion strategy is non-inferior to a liberal one in patients undergoing cardiac surgery.

trials.full_entry: TRISS (Holst et al., 2017): A restrictive transfusion strategy was non-inferior to a liberal strategy in patients with septic shock.
trials.description: A restrictive transfusion strategy was non-inferior to a liberal strategy in patients with septic shock.

trials.full_entry: VILLANUEVA (Villanueva et al., 2013): In patients with acute upper GI bleeding, a restrictive transfusion strategy (trigger <7 g/dL) resulted in higher survival rates than a liberal strategy (trigger <9 g/dL).]
trials.description: In patients with acute upper GI bleeding, a restrictive transfusion strategy (trigger <7 g/dL) resulted in higher survival rates than a liberal strategy (trigger <9 g/dL).]

trials.full_entry: AMIKINHAL (The AMIKINHAL study group, 2020): Inhaled amikacin as adjunctive therapy for VAP did not improve outcomes.
trials.description: Inhaled amikacin as adjunctive therapy for VAP did not improve outcomes.

trials.full_entry: BLING III (The BLING III study group, 2022): Continuous infusion of beta- lactam antibiotics was not superior to intermittent infusion in critically ill patients with sepsis.
trials.description: Continuous infusion of beta- lactam antibiotics was not superior to intermittent infusion in critically ill patients with sepsis.

trials.full_entry: Bozzette (1995): A foundational trial on the efficacy of different prophylactic regimens for Pneumocystis carinii pneumonia in HIV patients.
trials.description: A foundational trial on the efficacy of different prophylactic regimens for Pneumocystis carinii pneumonia in HIV patients.

trials.full_entry: COCA (The COCA trial group, 2021): In critically ill adults with influenza, oseltamivir was not associated with a reduction in mortality.
trials.description: In critically ill adults with influenza, oseltamivir was not associated with a reduction in mortality.

trials.full_entry: NIEWOEHNER (1999): A landmark trial showing the benefit of systemic glucocorticoids in acute exacerbations of COPD.
trials.description: A landmark trial showing the benefit of systemic glucocorticoids in acute exacerbations of COPD.

trials.full_entry: PRECISe (2019): A trial protocol to study prophylactic antibiotics in patients with predicted severe acute pancreatitis.
trials.description: A trial protocol to study prophylactic antibiotics in patients with predicted severe acute pancreatitis.

trials.full_entry: SuDDICU (Pettie et al., 2019): This Australian trial found no mortality benefit for Selective Decontamination of the Digestive Tract (SDD).
trials.description: This Australian trial found no mortality benefit for Selective Decontamination of the Digestive Tract (SDD).

trials.full_entry: TICACOS (2009): A trial of antibiotic de-escalation based on procalcitonin levels.
trials.description: A trial of antibiotic de-escalation based on procalcitonin levels.

trials.full_entry: WUNDERINK (Wunderink et al., 2012): Using procalcitonin levels to guide the duration of antibiotic therapy for community-acquired pneumonia was safe and reduced antibiotic exposure.
trials.description: Using procalcitonin levels to guide the duration of antibiotic therapy for community-acquired pneumonia was safe and reduced antibiotic exposure.

trials.full_entry: ACORN (2023): Compared a conservative versus liberal oxygenation strategy in mechanically ventilated adults.
trials.description: Compared a conservative versus liberal oxygenation strategy in mechanically ventilated adults.

trials.full_entry: ANZICS (Bellomo): Represents numerous landmark trials from the Australian and New Zealand Intensive Care Society (ANZICS) Clinical Trials Group.
trials.description: Represents numerous landmark trials from the Australian and New Zealand Intensive Care Society (ANZICS) Clinical Trials Group.

trials.full_entry: Bouchard (20005): A likely typo for Bouchard (2005), an important observational study identifying risk factors for AKI in the ICU.
trials.description: A likely typo for Bouchard (2005), an important observational study identifying risk factors for AKI in the ICU.

trials.full_entry: CAST (1991): The Cardiac Arrhythmia Suppression Trial, which found that certain antiarrhythmic drugs increased mortality after myocardial infarction.
trials.description: The Cardiac Arrhythmia Suppression Trial, which found that certain antiarrhythmic drugs increased mortality after myocardial infarction.

trials.full_entry: CATIS (2007), CATIS-1 (2014), CATIS-2 (2022): A series of trials investigating corticosteroid therapy for traumatic hyphema.
trials.description: A series of trials investigating corticosteroid therapy for traumatic hyphema.

trials.full_entry: Ethicus-2 (The Ethicus-2 study group, 2019): Systematic, proactive ethics consultations did not reduce the duration of organ support in ICU patients.
trials.description: Systematic, proactive ethics consultations did not reduce the duration of organ support in ICU patients.

trials.full_entry: FAST (2007): The Fluid and Catheter Treatment Trial, which assessed the utility of pulmonary artery catheters in heart failure management.
trials.description: The Fluid and Catheter Treatment Trial, which assessed the utility of pulmonary artery catheters in heart failure management.

trials.full_entry: IDEAL-ICU (The IDEAL-ICU study group, 2018): A systematic communication and end-of-life care strategy reduced anxiety and depression symptoms in family members.
trials.description: A systematic communication and end-of-life care strategy reduced anxiety and depression symptoms in family members.

trials.full_entry: IVOIRE (2010): A trial comparing intravenous versus oral rehydration in children with severe dehydration from gastroenteritis.
trials.description: A trial comparing intravenous versus oral rehydration in children with severe dehydration from gastroenteritis.

trials.full_entry: MASH-2 (2023): Assessed the role of methylprednisolone in treating patients with severe alcoholic hepatitis.
trials.description: Assessed the role of methylprednisolone in treating patients with severe alcoholic hepatitis.

trials.full_entry: NICO (2023): Studied early noninvasive ventilation in immunocompromised patients with acute respiratory failure.
trials.description: Studied early noninvasive ventilation in immunocompromised patients with acute respiratory failure.

trials.full_entry: OPTIMISE (2014): Assessed whether cardiac output-guided hemodynamic therapy improved outcomes in high-risk patients undergoing major abdominal surgery.
trials.description: Assessed whether cardiac output-guided hemodynamic therapy improved outcomes in high-risk patients undergoing major abdominal surgery.

trials.full_entry: PAC-MAN (2005): A UK-based trial that found no benefit from the use of a pulmonary artery catheter in critically ill patients.
trials.description: A UK-based trial that found no benefit from the use of a pulmonary artery catheter in critically ill patients.

trials.full_entry: PROTECTION (2023): Investigated the effect of intravenous amiodarone on the prevention of postoperative atrial fibrillation.
trials.description: Investigated the effect of intravenous amiodarone on the prevention of postoperative atrial fibrillation.

trials.full_entry: REMAP-CAP (Platform Trial): An ongoing adaptive platform trial investigating multiple interventions for community-acquired pneumonia and pandemics like COVID-19.
trials.description: An ongoing adaptive platform trial investigating multiple interventions for community-acquired pneumonia and pandemics like COVID-19.

trials.full_entry: REVISE (2023): A pediatric trial comparing restrictive versus liberal fluid resuscitation in septic shock.
trials.description: A pediatric trial comparing restrictive versus liberal fluid resuscitation in septic shock.

trials.full_entry: SIC (Vincent et al., 1995): The Sepsis in European Intensive Care study, a large observational study that provided key epidemiological data on sepsis.
trials.description: The Sepsis in European Intensive Care study, a large observational study that provided key epidemiological data on sepsis.

trials.full_entry: SORT (2022): A trial investigating the use of statin therapy in patients with sepsis.
trials.description: A trial investigating the use of statin therapy in patients with sepsis.

trials.full_entry: STOPAH (2015): A trial comparing prednisolone, pentoxifylline, or both for severe alcoholic hepatitis.
trials.description: A trial comparing prednisolone, pentoxifylline, or both for severe alcoholic hepatitis.

trials.full_entry: STROM (2015): Studied sorafenib as an adjuvant therapy for patients with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) after resection or ablation.
trials.description: Studied sorafenib as an adjuvant therapy for patients with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) after resection or ablation.
